content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 15, 2019

Shannon Eckmeyer
Assistant City Attorney II
City of Vallejo
555 Santa Clara Street, Third floor
Vallejo, CA 94590

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-19-018

Dear Ms. Eckmeyer:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTION

May Vallejo City Councilmembers McConnell and Brown take part in governmental decisions related to a potential General Plan Amendment, given that they own real property within 500 feet of districts that would be affected by the Amendment?

CONCLUSION

No, it is reasonably foreseeable the governmental decisions related to the General Plan Amendment, which would increase the permissible number of multifamily units in the affected districts, will have a material financial effect on the councilmembers' real property interests.

_______________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-19-018
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

The Vallejo City Council is considering a General Plan Amendment that would reduce the
minimum residential densities for selected General Plan Land Use Designations in order to facilitate
development of mixed-use and residential infill opportunities along the City's major corridors. The
reduced minimum density would allow more flexibility in the number of multifamily units that can
be developed on certain properties, allowing projects such as apartments or townhomes to be more
economically feasible. The zones that would be amended are the "District-North Gateway,"
"Central Corridor," and "Neighborhood Corridor," however, the affected districts could change.

In the City of Vallejo, a General Plan Amendment may be initiated either at the City
Council level or at the Planning Commission level. The City's current plan is to have the City
Council consider a resolution of intent to amend the General Plan and, if approved, the item would
then go to the Planning Commission for their recommendation. Ultimately, the General Plan
Amendment would be adopted by the City Council at a later date. If the City Council recommends
the process for an amendment be initiated, staff will analyze whether the amendment would
increase traffic in the affected districts. Development could either negatively or positively impact
connections to bicycle routes and pedestrian connections, depending on project proposals.

Councilmember McConnell owns more than a dozen properties within the City. One of
these properties is located within 500 feet of a district that would be affected by the General Plan
Amendment. The property is a triplex with units that Councilmember McConnell rents out.
Councilmember Brown has four properties within affected districts. Three of the properties are used
for commercial/industrial purposes, while the fourth property is Councilmember Brown's residence.

ANALYSIS

Interests

Under Section 87100, "[n]o public official at any level of state or local government shall
make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest." A
public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from
its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her immediate family, or on an
enumerated interest. (Section 87103.) Such interests include "[a]ny real property in which the
public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more."
(Section 87103(b).) Here, both councilmembers have economic interests in their real properties,
each of which is presumably worth well over $2,000.

Foreseeability and Materiality

A financial effect on a financial interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the
financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official
or the official's agency. (Regulation 1870l(a).) Regarding financial interests not explicitly involved
in a decision, a financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In
general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical

File No. A-19-018
Page No. 3

or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial result cannot be expected absent
extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not reasonably
foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).)

Regulation 18702.2 provides that the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
governmental decision on a real property in which an official has a financial interest is material
under certain enumerated circumstances. The regulation provides that the effect is material
whenever the governmental decision "[i]nvolves property located 500 feet or less from the property
line of the parcel unless there is clear and convincing evidence that the decision will not have any
measurable impact on the official's property . . . ." (Regulation 18702.2(a)(7)2.)

No facts have been provided that clearly and convincingly establish the governmental
decisions at issue would not have any measurable impact on the properties owned by
Councilmembers Brown and McConnell, all of which are within 500 feet of affected districts. To
the contrary, the purpose of the General Plan Amendment is to allow for an increase in the number
of multifamily properties, such as apartments and townhomes, throughout the affected districts.
This would likely increase the amount of available housing and affect the market value, income
producing potential, and development potential of the properties. Accordingly, the reasonably
foreseeable financial effects on the councilmembers' real property interests are material. Neither
councilmember may take part in governmental decisions related to the General Plan Amendment.3

The "Public Generally" Exception

Under Regulation 18703, a governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's
financial interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the official establishes
that a significant segment of the public is affected and the effect on his or her financial interest is
not unique compared to the effect on the significant segment. (Regulation 18703(a).) A significant
segment of the public includes at least 25 percent of all real property, commercial real property, or
residential real property within the official's jurisdiction. (Regulation 18703(b).) No facts have been
provided indicating that the General Plan Amendment would affect a significant segment of the
public and, thus, the "public generally" exception does not appear to apply.

Segmentation

If the General Plan Amendment decisions can be broken down into separate decisions that
are not inextricably interrelated to a decision in which the councilmembers have disqualifying
conflicts of interest, they may take part in the decisions unrelated to their economic interests, so
long as the requirements of Regulation 18706(a) are satisfied. All of the following conditions must
apply:

________________________
2 Citation provided is to Regulation 18702.2 as amended by the Commission on January 17, 2019.

3 Under Regulation 18702.2(a)(1), the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a governmental decision on a
parcel of real property in which an official has a financial interest, other than a leasehold interest, is also material
whenever the governmental decision involves the adoption of or amendment to a development plan or criteria applying
to the parcel. Though potentially applicable, we do not analyze this subdivision here, given that the councilmembers are
already disqualified from the governmental decisions under Regulation 18702.2(a)(7).

File No. A-19-018
Page No. 4

(1) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision in which the official has a disqualifying financial interest;

(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from the other decisions;

(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official's participation in any way; and

(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made, the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening of, or otherwise financial affect, the decision from which the official was disqualified.

(Regulation 18706(a).)⁴

As noted, Regulation 18706(a)(3) provides that the decision in which an official has a financial interest be considered first. If the five properties at issue do not fall within the same affected district, multiple segmented decisions would be required. When there is more than one segmented decision, each must be determined in an order that would prevent the result of one segmented decision from determining or altering the result of the other(s). (See Murphy Advice Letter, Nos. A-07-031 & A-07-050.) In short, the decision in which the official has a financial interest must be decided before the official is able to take part in subsequent governmental decisions.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Kevin Cornwall
Counsel, Legal Division

KMC:jgl

_________________________

⁴ Regulation 18706(c) provides, "[o]nce all the separate decisions related to a … general plan affecting the entire jurisdiction have been finalized, the public official may participate in the final vote to … adopt, reject, or amend the general plan." Here, however, the General Plan Amendment pertains to a limited number of districts within the jurisdiction, not the entire jurisdiction itself. Accordingly, Regulation 18706(c) is inapplicable in this context.

500 feet of districts that would be affected by the Amendment?

Amendment, which would increase the permissible number of multifamily units in the affected 
districts, will have a material financial effect on the councilmembers' real property interests.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May Vallejo City Councilmembers McConnell and Brown take part in governmental 
decisions related to a potential General Plan Amendment, given that they own real property within 
500 feet of districts that would be affected by the Amendment?

CONCLUSION: No, it is reasonably foreseeable the governmental decisions related to the General Plan 
Amendment, which would increase the permissible number of multifamily units in the affected 
districts, will have a material financial effect on the councilmembers' real property interests.
